steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence (quantitative) question. The table shows percentages of New Year's resolution makers in four age brackets who make each type of resolution. The task is to find the conclusion that is directly supported by the table numbers — no outside inference allowed.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The majority of US adults who make resolutions related to health and exercise also make resolutions in multiple additional categories.: The table shows health-and-exercise percentages by age group among resolution makers, but says nothing about how many categories any individual person also chose. This claim cannot be verified from the table. ✗
  • Choice B — Resolution makers between the ages of 50 and 64 are more likely to make resolutions related to personal relationships and less likely to make resolutions related to finances than resolution makers between the ages of 30 and 49 are.: Personal relationships: 58% (age 50–64) vs. 53% (age 30–49) — 50–64 is higher. Finances: 56% (age 50–64) vs. 63% (age 30–49) — 50–64 is lower. Both parts of the claim check out exactly. ✓
  • Choice C — Among all US adults, people become less likely to make New Year's resolutions as they age, regardless of the type of resolution.: The table covers only people who already make resolutions, not all US adults. Also, health-and-exercise percentages do not steadily decline with age (79, 80, 79, 76). ✗
  • Choice D — Resolution makers between the ages of 18 and 29 are more likely to make resolutions about health and exercise than resolution makers between the ages of 30 and 49 are.: Health and exercise: 79% (18–29) vs. 80% (30–49) — the 18–29 group is actually lower, not higher. ✗
That is the correct answer.
